startScreenOptions.title: AN14.1-AN14.4 — Interactive Lecture (AN)
startScreenOptions.shortStartDescription: Interactive lecture with 3 embedded questions for AN14.1-AN14.4.

interactions.label: A fracture of the femoral neck lying within the joint capsule carries a high ris

params.question: A fracture of the femoral neck lying within the joint capsule carries a high risk of avascular necrosis. What is the primary reason for this complication?

answers.text: The fracture prevents synovial fluid from nourishing the articular cartilage of the femoral head

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. While synovial fluid nourishes articular cartilage, avascular necrosis refers to bone death from loss of blood supply, not cartilage nutrition. The key issue is disruption of blood vessels that run within the joint capsule.

answers.text: The fracture disrupts blood vessels running within the joint capsule that supply the femoral head

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! The blood vessels supplying the femoral head travel within the joint capsule. An intracapsular fracture can sever these vessels completely, cutting off blood supply to the femoral head and causing avascular necrosis — death of bone tissue from ischaemia.

answers.text: The fracture compresses the femoral artery as it passes beneath the inguinal ligament

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The femoral artery passes well away from the fracture site. Avascular necrosis occurs because the fracture disrupts the smaller vessels running within the joint capsule that are the femoral head's primary blood supply.

answers.text: The hip joint capsule prevents inflammatory cells from reaching the fracture site for repair

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The capsule does not block inflammation. The concern is vascular — intracapsular fractures disrupt blood vessels within the capsule that supply the femoral head, leading to bone death from ischaemia.

overallFeedback.feedback: The key concept is that blood vessels supplying the femoral head travel within the joint capsule. An intracapsular fracture can tear these vessels, completely cutting off blood supply and causing avascular necrosis.
Well done! Intracapsular fractures are dangerous precisely because they can disrupt the blood supply running within the capsule, leaving the femoral head avascular.

interactions.label: A radiologist examining a newborn's knee X-ray identifies the lower femoral epip

params.question: A radiologist examining a newborn's knee X-ray identifies the lower femoral epiphysis. What clinical conclusion can be drawn from its presence?

answers.text: The infant has a normal calcium and vitamin D metabolism

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The presence of the lower femoral epiphysis indicates gestational maturity, not metabolic status. This ossification centre appears at approximately 36 weeks of foetal life and confirms term gestation.

answers.text: The infant's growth plates are about to close prematurely

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Growth plate closure occurs much later, in adolescence. The lower femoral epiphysis appears at around 36 weeks of foetal life, and its presence is used to confirm that the newborn has reached term gestation.

answers.text: The infant has reached at least 36 weeks of gestational age

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Correct! The lower femoral epiphysis (distal femoral ossification centre) appears in the ninth month of foetal life, around 36 weeks of gestation. Its presence on X-ray is a reliable indicator that the newborn has reached term.

answers.text: The infant has completed skeletal maturation of the lower limb

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. Skeletal maturation of the lower limb takes years to complete. The lower femoral epiphysis appears in the ninth month of foetal life and its presence simply indicates that the baby has reached at least 36 weeks of gestation.

overallFeedback.feedback: The lower femoral epiphysis appears in the ninth foetal month (around 36 weeks). Its presence on a newborn's X-ray indicates that the infant has reached term gestation — a fact used in forensic medicine and neonatology.
Excellent! The lower femoral epiphysis is one of the few ossification centres present at birth and serves as a forensic and clinical marker of term gestation (≥36 weeks).

metadata.title: A radiologist examining a newborn's knee

interactions.label: Which tarsal bone is unique in having no muscle attachments, because nearly its

params.question: Which tarsal bone is unique in having no muscle attachments, because nearly its entire surface is covered by articular cartilage?

tipsAndFeedback.chosenFeedback: Incorrect. The calcaneus receives the Achilles tendon posteriorly and gives origin to several plantar muscles. The tarsal bone with no muscle attachments is the talus, whose surface is almost entirely articular cartilage.

Incorrect. The navicular tuberosity serves as the insertion point for the tibialis posterior muscle. The talus is the tarsal bone devoid of muscle attachments because its surface is almost entirely articular.

Incorrect. The cuboid has a groove on its plantar surface that carries the peroneus longus tendon. The talus is the unique tarsal bone with no muscle attachments due to its extensive articular cartilage coverage.

Correct! The talus articulates with the tibia, fibula, calcaneus, and navicular. Nearly all of its surface is covered by articular cartilage for these joints, leaving no surface area for muscle attachment.

overallFeedback.feedback: The talus is unique among tarsal bones: it articulates with the tibia, fibula, calcaneus, and navicular. Nearly its entire surface is articular cartilage, so no muscle can attach to it.
Well done! The talus is remarkable — it articulates with four bones and has nearly all of its surface covered by articular cartilage, leaving no room for muscle attachment.
